intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid transitions over instant page swaps when tracking experiments over time
facade.axisTargeted: Motion continuity vs instant state change
facade.label: Smooth slides between screens
facade.acceptImplies: Build transition animations into dashboard navigation; prioritize fluid motion between different experiment views and time periods
facade.rejectImplies: Keep navigation instant and snappy; minimize animations; optimize for quick, click-to-answer interactions without motion overhead

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards face a primary choice between chronological/temporal visualization (timeline, stream) vs modular snapshot display (card grid). This axis determines information architecture depth.
facade.label: Timeline vs cards
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward sequential, time-based narrative flow for tracking experiments—prefers seeing progression and cause-effect over moment snapshots.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers modular, independent metric views—wants to compare current state across dimensions without enforcing temporal reading order.

facade.hypothesis: A conversational, personality-driven tone with celebratory language will feel more motivating than clinical data presentation for personal health tracking.
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality: Friendly vs Professional
facade.label: Chatty progress updates
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers warm, encouraging, conversational language—voice should feel like a supportive friend commenting on their experiments, not a medical dashboard.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers straightforward, understated communication—voice should be neutral and objective, letting data speak for itself without motivational language or personality.

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid transitions over instant page swaps when tracking experiments over time
evidence.implication: Build transition animations into dashboard navigation; prioritize fluid motion between different experiment views and time periods

facade.hypothesis: A fixed left sidebar for experiment navigation paired with card-based layouts in the main area will feel organized and scannable for health tracking
facade.axisTargeted: Information architecture: sidebar vs tabs; card-based vs list-based content organization
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers vertical navigation paired with modular, contained content blocks—suggests they want clear sections without top-level tabs, and like visual grouping of related data
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer horizontal navigation (tabs), linear scrolling layouts, or dense list views—suggests they want less spatial division or faster sequential browsing over spatial organization

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard with desaturated, warm palette (warm grays, muted sage, soft cream) will feel more calming and intimate than bright or cool tones, supporting reflection without overstimulation.
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool + calm vs energetic
facade.label: Soft, muted colors
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers a contemplative, non-jarring visual environment for health tracking—likely values restraint and gentleness over visual pop.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer more vibrancy, clarity, or energetic feedback in their dashboard—possibly values immediate visual feedback and distinction over ambient calm.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer gentle, approachable interface elements over sharp edges for a health dashboard that feels supportive rather than clinical
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Pursue gentle, rounded aesthetic throughout cards, buttons, and containers. Avoid hard angles. Consider this a trust-building signal for vulnerable health data.
facade.rejectImplies: Users may prefer a cleaner, more minimal or technical look. Sharp edges, flat geometry, or a more austere visual language might feel more trustworthy for quantified health tracking.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments over time prefer discrete, tappable checkpoints (instant jumps between dates/weeks) rather than continuous scrolling through a chronological feed
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation granularity — discrete jumps vs fluid scrolling
facade.label: Tap to jump vs swipe to scroll
facade.content: Tap to jump vs swipe to scroll
facade.acceptImplies: Design with date/week/month picker buttons or step-through controls; prioritize quick access to specific time periods over endless scroll
facade.rejectImplies: Build continuous scrollable timeline or feed; make temporal navigation seamless and momentum-based rather than modal or discrete

agent.focus: "Tap to jump vs swipe to scroll"
